steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The sentence ends: "...in fellow OECD member ______ South Korea." The country name "South Korea" is a restrictive appositive — it identifies which member nation is meant. Restrictive appositives attach directly to the noun they rename with no punctuation.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — nation: South Korea.: "...in fellow OECD member nation: South Korea." A colon after "nation" wrongly implies a list or definition is following; colons don't introduce a single-word renaming appositive. ✗
  • Choice B — nation South Korea.: "...in fellow OECD member nation South Korea." No punctuation — correct for a restrictive appositive that identifies which nation. ✓
  • Choice C — nation, South Korea.: "...in fellow OECD member nation, South Korea." A comma signals a non-restrictive appositive, implying there is only one OECD member nation; but the passage establishes there are 38 members, so the name is essential. ✗
  • Choice D — nation—South Korea.: "...in fellow OECD member nation—South Korea." An em dash opens a parenthetical insert, which would need a closing em dash before the period; none appears. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
